title: Meter Cross-Check
learningObjectives: Correlate measured voltage and current with calculated values
Maintain circuit validity while adding meters
Use resistance to keep measurements in readable ranges
introText: Meters only help when circuits are solid. You'll build a loop, add meters, and cross-check readings against your calculations.

steps.instructionText: Create a closed series loop with a 9V source and a 390Ω resistor.
steps.feedbackOnFailure: Ensure the source and resistor are wired end-to-end with no open terminals.

steps.instructionText: Check that the current is around 23 mA and stays below 25 mA.
steps.feedbackOnSuccess: Current is under 25 mA — your loop is within the expected range.
steps.feedbackOnFailure: Adjust resistance upward or lower voltage until current drops below 25 mA.

steps.instructionText: Add both an ammeter and voltmeter to visualize readings without breaking the circuit.
steps.feedbackOnSuccess: Meters are present and the source still provides about 9V. Readings align with your calculations.
steps.feedbackOnFailure: Keep the source near 9V and ensure adding meters didn’t break the loop.

questions.questionText: What must be true before trusting meter readings?
options: The circuit is valid and wired as intended
All resistors are set to zero
questions.explanation: Meters only reflect reality when the circuit is correctly wired and valid.

questions.questionText: Why keep current under 25 mA during measurement?
options: Lower current reduces heat and protects parts
questions.explanation: Staying under 25 mA avoids overheating small components and keeps sensors safe.
